root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Case Audits Guide
root.summary: Front matter and Chapter 1 of the Cúram 8.1.2 Case Audits Guide. Covers what case audits are and why they’re used, distinguishing quantitative audits (compliance/statute/policy) from qualitative audits (case outcomes and performance). Explains audit roles: audit coordinator, auditor, and audit team, plus the audit lifecycle from plan creation to findings and recommendations. Describes audit anatomy, including focus areas, selection queries/criteria, and audit algorithms, and how audits are configured by case type, permissions, and security. Also covers coordinator workflows for creating, maintaining, and tracking audit plans, and auditor workflows for searching, completing audits, recording findings, and managing feedback from case owners/supervisors. Includes notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and legal terms.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Brief introductory note for the document. It tells readers to review the Notices section on page 21 before using the information or the supported product, and includes the Merative US L.P. copyright notice for 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram Case Audits guide: applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2. Includes copyright/trademark notices and a table of contents, then starts Chapter 1 “Cúram Case Audits overview.” The chapter introduces case audits, explains quantitative vs qualitative audits, audit roles (coordinator, auditor, audit team), audit anatomy (focus areas, selection queries, algorithms), lifecycle stages, configuration options, security, and begins coordinating audits and creating audit plans.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-20
children.summary: This section overviews Cúram Case Audits. It covers audit plans, focus areas, selecting cases, adding auditors/teams, assigning and reassigning cases, changing coordinators, searching and tracking progress, and summary findings. It also explains case audit execution: auditor home page options, addressing focus areas, entering findings, searching/viewing audit progress, completion statuses, and case owner/supervisor feedback workflows. Includes user involvement modes: provide feedback, provide feedback only when a focus area is not satisfied, view findings only, or no access.

Front matter for the Cúram Case Audits guide, applying to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, with copyright/trademark notices and contents. Begins Chapter 1 on Cúram Case Audits overview: what case audits are, quantitative vs qualitative audits, audit roles (coordinator, auditor, team), audit anatomy (focus areas, selection queries, algorithms), lifecycle stages, and configuration/security. Also covers coordinating audits and auditing cases: creating/managing plans, assigning/reassigning cases, tracking progress, summary findings, auditor workflows, and case owner/supervisor feedback/status handling.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: States that the document’s sample data and names are fictitious, and that sample programs may be copied, modified, and distributed under the Merative Client Agreement for compatible application development. Notes the samples are untested and provided “AS IS” without warranty or liability. Includes a link to Merative’s privacy policy and a trademarks notice listing Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and other marks.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: This section lists Merative and third-party trademarks used in the document, including IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX. It also notes the sample data and programs are fictional/example-only, provides the Merative privacy policy link, and states copyright/license terms for sample source programs, including “AS IS” warranty disclaimers.

This notices section sets the legal terms for using the publication: personal and internal commercial reuse is allowed only if proprietary notices are preserved, but redistribution, derivative works, and external use require Merative’s consent. It disclaims warranties, limits rights, and notes export-law compliance. It also states Merative may withdraw permission, mentions possible patents, and says non-Merative product information comes from third parties and is untested.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Case Audits guide. Lists front matter (Note, Edition) and chapter 1 on case audits, including overview, configuring audits, coordinating audits, and auditing cases. Also shows subtopics such as case audit types and roles, anatomy and lifecycle of a case audit, audit plan creation/maintenance/progress, summary findings, and completing a case audit, plus notices, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: 1 Cúram Case Audits overview
1.1 Overview of case audits
Case audit types and roles
children.summary: This section introduces Cúram case audits and explains that they monitor benefit/service delivery through two audit types: quantitative and qualitative. Quantitative audits assess compliance with statutes and policy using case data, while qualitative audits evaluate case outcomes using auditor judgment and may include interviews. It also notes audits can combine both elements and that organizational roles include an audit coordinator and an auditor, which may be held by the same user.

children.title: Anatomy of a case audit
children.summary: Explains the roles in case auditing: audit coordinator, auditor, and audit team, including permissions and business-area limits. Describes key audit anatomy elements: focus areas selected from admin-defined options, selection queries and criteria for generating random case samples (dynamic vs. fixed), and the audit algorithm that produces the sample. Useful for understanding how audits are planned, assigned, and executed.

children.title: Case audit lifecycle
children.summary: This section outlines the case audit lifecycle and setup basics. It describes the main stages: create an audit plan, produce a case sample list, assign auditors, record audit findings, and record summary findings/recommendations. It also introduces configuration options for case audits, including defining case types, selecting an audit algorithm, choosing case selection queries, and setting focus areas, permissions, and security. A sample algorithm is mentioned that selects every nth case from a starting point and interval.

Introduces Cúram case audits and their purpose: monitoring delivery of benefits and services through quantitative audits (compliance with statutes/policy) and qualitative audits (case outcomes, sometimes including interviews). Explains audit roles: audit coordinator, auditor, and audit team, including business-area limits and that one user can hold both coordinator and auditor roles. Also outlines core audit anatomy: configurable focus areas, selection queries/criteria for random case samples (dynamic or fixed), and the audit algorithm that generates the sample.

children.title: 1.2 Configuring case audits
children.summary: Explains how administrators configure case audits by case type, selection algorithm, focus areas, and queries. Describes the audit algorithm used to generate random samples, including the provided “every nth case” method and custom algorithm options. Covers how focus areas are made available per case type. Details selection queries: one required dynamic query per configuration plus optional fixed SQL-based queries. Notes supported case types and that new queries or algorithms may require development and validation.

children.title: 1.3 Coordinating case audits
Personalizing the audit coordinator home page
children.summary: This section explains how audit coordinators personalize their home page by choosing widgets such as Quicklinks, My Tasks, My Items of Interest, My Audit Plans, My Case Audits, and summary charts for plans and audits. It also covers creating an audit plan: selecting the case type, purpose, priority, focus areas, and level of user involvement. The section notes that case types must be configured first, cases can be generated randomly or added manually if allowed, and cases must then be assigned to auditors.

children.title: Creating an audit plan
children.summary: Explains how to create an audit plan in Cúram. An audit plan defines the case audit process from start to finish and requires selecting the case type, audit purpose, priority, focus areas, and user involvement level. Case types must be configured in administration first. Cases are added by scheduling the plan and generating a random case list, or manually adding cases if allowed, then assigning them to auditors. Also notes configuring the audit coordinator home page with items like tasks, plans, case audits, and summary views.

children.title: Maintaining an audit plan
children.summary: Explains how to maintain an audit plan: define the audit period, generate or select cases, add qualified auditors or audit teams, and assign cases. The plan must be scheduled before cases can be assigned, and status changes from Pending to In Progress on first assignment. Covers reassignment/removal of case audits, notifications to auditors and case owners/supervisors, changing the audit coordinator with security restrictions, and recording summary findings after audits are completed.

children.title: Viewing audit plan progress
children.summary: Explains how audit plan progress is monitored in Cúram Case Audits. Audit coordinators can view progress graphs on their home page and within the audit plan, with bar chart or pie chart display options. Home page graphs summarize audit plans they coordinate and case audits they are assigned. The audit plan shows case audit status and focus area progress, including satisfied, not satisfied, and not yet examined counts, with hover details. It also covers notifications for completed case audits and how coordinators add summary findings and mark the audit plan complete.

children.title: Maintaining audit plan summary findings
children.summary: Explains how an audit coordinator maintains an audit plan by using completion notifications from case audits. After each case audit is finished, the coordinator is notified and can open the case audit to review findings and feedback. When all case audits are complete, a second notification lets the coordinator navigate to the audit plan, enter summary findings, and then mark the audit plan complete.

Covers the full audit-plan lifecycle for case audits: personalizing the audit coordinator home page, creating and scheduling plans, selecting case types, focus areas, priority, and user involvement, and adding cases and auditors/teams. Explains manual vs random case selection, assignment/reassignment/removal of case audits, coordinator change, security restrictions, search, and notifications to auditors and case owners/supervisors. Also describes progress monitoring via home-page and plan graphs, focus-area status, and recording summary findings before marking the audit plan complete.

children.title: 1.4 Auditing cases
Completing a case audit
Providing case owner and supervisor feedback
children.summary: Explains how audit feedback is assigned and completed when an audit plan uses “Provide Feedback” or “Provide Feedback When Focus Area Not Satisfied.” Case owner and supervisor each get a task to review audit findings and submit feedback; the task closes automatically when feedback is complete. If the case owner cannot respond, the supervisor may provide feedback on their behalf. The auditor tracks completion and can mark the audit complete, moving statuses from “Feedback Received” to “Feedback Complete” and finally “Complete.”

children.title: Indicating that a case audit is complete
children.summary: Explains how a case audit is marked complete after feedback from the case owner and/or supervisor. The auditor stays responsible until feedback is received, then sets the audit to Complete. If feedback comes from one party, the audit status becomes Feedback Received; if both complete feedback, it becomes Feedback Complete; once the auditor confirms completion, it becomes Complete. Also includes Merative legal notices, copyright, privacy policy, and trademark information.

Explains how to complete a case audit in Cúram. It covers searching for audits, viewing audit progress on auditor and case audit home pages, and how completion depends on the audit plan’s user involvement level. If the case owner/supervisor has no access or only view findings is required, the audit can be marked complete directly. If feedback is required, the case owner and supervisor receive tasks, submit feedback, and the audit moves through Feedback Received/Feedback Complete before the auditor sets it to Complete. Includes Merative notices and legal information.

Explains Cúram case audits: how auditors perform audits by addressing focus areas, adding findings, and completing the audit. Covers auditor home page personalization, notifications, search options, and graphical progress views. Describes completion logic based on audit plan user involvement: no access or “View Findings only” lets the auditor mark the audit complete directly; “Provide Feedback” modes send tasks to case owner/supervisor, move through Feedback Received/Feedback Complete, and then the auditor can finish the audit. Includes product notices/legal text.

Overview of Cúram case audits: purpose, quantitative vs. qualitative audits, and roles for audit coordinator, auditor, and audit team. Explains audit anatomy (focus areas, selection queries/criteria, audit algorithm) and lifecycle from creating plans to recording findings and recommendations. Covers configuration by case type, focus areas, dynamic/fixed selection queries, and custom algorithms. Describes auditor workflow, search and progress views, completion states, and feedback handling from case owners/supervisors. Includes legal notices and trademarks.
